title: Charmed Life — 4 (Fragment 4)
sourceText:
“And we’ve both inherited it,” said Julia. “I must go and wash.” She picked up three slices of bread to keep her going while she did so, and left the room, calling over her shoulder, “Tell Michael I won’t be long, Roger.”

“More cocoa?” Roger said politely, picking up the jug.

“Yes, please,” said Cat. It never bothered him to eat or drink things that had been bewitched, and he was thirsty. He thought that if he filled his mouth with marmalade and strained the cocoa through it, he might not taste the cocoa. Gwendolen, however, was sure Roger was trying to insult her. She flounced around in her chair and stayed haughtily looking at the wall until Mr. Saunders suddenly threw open a door Cat had not noticed before and said cheerfully:

“Right, all of you. Lesson time. Come on through and see how you stand up to some grilling.”

Cat hastily swallowed his cocoa-flavored marmalade. Beyond the door was a schoolroom. It was a real, genuine schoolroom, although there were only four desks in it. There was a blackboard, a globe, the pitted school floor, and the schoolroom smell. There was that kind of glass-fronted bookcase without which no schoolroom is complete, and the battered gray-green and dark blue books without which no schoolroom bookcase is complete. On the walls were big pictures of the statues Mr. Saunders had found so interesting.

Two of the desks were brown and old. Two were new and yellow with varnish. Gwendolen and Cat sat silently in the new desks. Julia hurried in, with her face shining from soap, and sat in the old desk beside Roger’s, and the grilling began. Mr. Saunders strode gawkily up and down in front of the blackboard, asking keen questions. His tweed jacket billowed out from his back, just as his coat had done in the wind. Perhaps that was why the sleeves of the jacket were so much too short for Mr. Saunders’ long arms. The long arm shot out, and a foot of bony wrist with a keen finger on the end of it pointed at Cat. “What part did witchcraft play in the Wars of the Roses?”

“Er,” said Cat. “Ung. I’m afraid I haven’t done them yet, sir.”

“Gwendolen,” said Mr. Saunders.

“Oh—a very big part,” Gwendolen guessed airily.

“Wrong,” said Mr. Saunders. “Roger.”

From the grilling, it emerged that Roger and Julia had forgotten a great deal over the summer but, even so, they were well ahead of Cat in most things, and far ahead of Gwendolen in everything.

“What did you learn at school?” Mr. Saunders asked her, in some exasperation.

Gwendolen shrugged. “I’ve forgotten. It wasn’t interesting. I was concentrating on witchcraft, and I intend to go on doing that, please.”

“I’m afraid you can’t,” said Mr. Saunders.

Gwendolen stared at him, hardly able to believe she had heard him right. “What!” she almost shrieked. “But—but I’m terribly talented! I have to go on with it!”

“Your talents will keep,” said Mr. Saunders. “You can take up witchcraft again when you’ve learned something else. Open your arithmetic book and do me the first four exercises. Eric, I think I’ll set you going on some History. Write me an essay on the reign of King Canute.” He moved on to set work for Roger and Julia.

words.definition: To receive (property, a title, etc.) from an ancestor or predecessor by legal succession.
words.example: And we’ve both inherited it.
words.exampleText: And we’ve both inherited it.

words.definition: Under a spell; enchanted or magically influenced.
words.example: He never bothered to eat or drink things that had been bewitched.
words.exampleText: He never bothered him to eat or drink things that had been bewitched.

words.definition: To move impatiently or angrily, often with exaggerated movements.
words.example: She flounced around in her chair and stayed haughtily looking at the wall.
words.exampleText: She flounced around in her chair and stayed haughtily looking at the wall.

words.definition: In a proud or arrogant manner, showing superiority or disdain.
words.example: Gwendolen stayed haughtily looking at the wall.
words.exampleText: She stayed haughtily looking at the wall.

words.definition: In a happy and optimistic way.
words.example: Mr. Saunders suddenly threw open a door and said cheerfully, “Right, all of you.”
words.exampleText: ...and said cheerfully: “Right, all of you.”

words.definition: A thorough and intense questioning or interrogation.
words.example: Come on through and see how you stand up to some grilling.
words.exampleText: ...see how you stand up to some grilling.

words.definition: Marked with many small holes or scars.
words.example: There was a blackboard, a globe, the pitted school floor.
words.exampleText: There was the pitted school floor.

words.definition: Authentic, real, not fake or counterfeit.
words.example: It was a real, genuine schoolroom.
words.exampleText: It was a real, genuine schoolroom.

words.definition: Worn out or damaged by continuous use or age.
words.example: The battered gray-green and dark blue books...
words.exampleText: The battered gray-green and dark blue books.

words.definition: Three-dimensional figures sculpted typically from stone or metal.
words.example: On the walls were big pictures of the statues Mr. Saunders had found interesting.
words.exampleText: Big pictures of the statues.

words.definition: Walked with long, decisive steps.
words.example: Mr. Saunders strode gawkily up and down in front of the blackboard.
words.exampleText: Mr. Saunders strode gawkily up and down.

words.definition: In an awkward or clumsy manner.
words.example: Mr. Saunders strode gawkily up and down.
words.exampleText: Mr. Saunders strode gawkily up and down.

words.definition: Swelled out in waves or surges.
words.example: His tweed jacket billowed out from his back.
words.exampleText: His tweed jacket billowed out from his back.

words.definition: Showing enthusiasm or intellect; sharp or intense.
words.example: Asking keen questions.
words.exampleText: Asking keen questions.

words.definition: A feeling of intense irritation or annoyance.
words.example: Mr. Saunders asked in some exasperation.
words.exampleText: Asked her in some exasperation.

words.definition: Uttered a high-pitched piercing sound or cry, usually as an expression of fear, pain, or excitement.
words.example: She almost shrieked in surprise.
words.exampleText: She almost shrieked.

words.definition: Having a natural aptitude or skill for something.
words.example: But I’m terribly talented!
words.exampleText: I’m terribly talented!

words.definition: The branch of mathematics dealing with addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.
words.example: Open your arithmetic book and do the exercises.
words.exampleText: Open your arithmetic book and do me the first four exercises.

words.definition: A short piece of writing on a particular subject, often expressing personal views.
words.example: Write me an essay on the reign of King Canute.
words.exampleText: Write me an essay on the reign of King Canute.

words.definition: The period during which a sovereign rules.
words.example: The reign of King Canute.
words.exampleText: Write me an essay on the reign of King Canute.

words.definition: Focusing all one's attention on a particular object or activity.
words.example: I was concentrating on witchcraft.
words.exampleText: I was concentrating on witchcraft.

words.definition: Instruction to begin activities in the arithmetic textbook.
words.example: Open your arithmetic book and do me the first four exercises.
words.exampleText: Open your arithmetic book.

words.definition: To endure or handle a rigorous, intensive questioning.
words.example: Come on through and see how you stand up to some grilling.
words.exampleText: See how you stand up to some grilling.

words.definition: To continue doing an activity or task.
words.example: I intend to go on doing witchcraft.
words.exampleText: I intend to go on doing that.

words.definition: Failed to remember.
words.example: Roger and Julia had forgotten a great deal over the summer.
words.exampleText: They had forgotten a great deal over the summer.
